questions.question: Which part of the Constitution of India declares the ideals of the welfare state?
questions.solution:
The Directive Principles of State Policy (Part IV) declare the ideals of a Welfare State.
  • Unlike the colonial 'Police State', a Welfare State aims to promote the socio-economic well-being of its citizens.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
  1. The Constitution of India defines its basic structure in terms of federalism, secularism, fundamental rights and democracy
  2. The Constitution of India provides for the 'judicial review' to safeguard the citizen's liberties and to preserve the ideals on which the Constitution is based
Which of the above-given statements is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Both statements are incorrect regarding the literal text of the Constitution:
  • Statement 1: The 'Basic Structure' is a judicial innovation (Kesavananda Bharati case); it is not defined anywhere in the Constitution.
  • Statement 2: While the Constitution provides the basis for Judicial Review (Articles 13, 32, 226), the term 'Judicial Review' itself is not explicitly mentioned in the text of the Constitution.

questions.question: The Preamble to the Constitution of India is
questions.option_1: A part of the constitution but has no legal effect
questions.option_2: Not a part of the constitution and has no legal effect either
questions.option_3: A part of the constitution and has the same legal effect as any other part
questions.option_4: A part of the constitution but has no legal effect independently of other parts
questions.solution: In the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973), the Supreme Court ruled that the Preamble is a part of the Constitution. However, it is non-justiciable, meaning it has no legal effect independently of the other parts (it is used as a guiding light to interpret other provisions).

questions.question:
With reference to the provisions contained in Part IV of the Constitution of India, which of the following statements is/are correct?
  1. They shall be enforceable by the courts
  2. They shall not be enforceable by any courts
  3. The principles laid down in this part are to influence the making of laws by the state
Select the correct answer using the given code below:
questions.solution:
Part IV (DPSP) characteristics are:
  • Statement 1: Incorrect. They are non-enforceable by courts (Article 37).
  • Statement 2: Correct. Article 37 explicitly states they shall not be enforceable by any court.
  • Statement 3: Correct. Article 37 also states that these principles are fundamental in the governance of the country and the State must apply them in making laws.

questions.question: In the context of polity, which one of the following would you accept as the most appropriate definition of liberty?
questions.option_1: Protection against the tyranny of political rulers
questions.option_3: Opportunity to do whatever one likes
questions.solution:
In political philosophy, while liberty can mean 'absence of restraint', its most positive and 'appropriate' definition in a modern polity is the 'Opportunity to develop oneself fully'.
  • Absence of restraint is 'Negative Liberty', whereas providing an environment for self-growth is 'Positive Liberty'.

questions.question: The Ninth Schedule was introduced in the Constitution of India during the prime ministership of
questions.solution:
The Ninth Schedule was added to the Constitution by the 1st Amendment Act, 1951, during the tenure of Jawaharlal Nehru as Prime Minister.
  • It was created to protect land reform laws and other laws from being challenged in the courts on the grounds of violating Fundamental Rights.
  • While later PMs like Indira Gandhi added many laws to it, the introduction of the schedule itself happened under Nehru to bypass judicial hurdles in agrarian reforms.

questions.question: Which Article of the Constitution of India safeguards one's right to marry the person of one's choice?
questions.solution:
The Right to Marry a person of one's choice is considered an integral part of the Right to Life and Personal Liberty under Article 21.
  • The Supreme Court reaffirmed this in the Hadiya Case (Shafin Jahan v. Asokan K.M., 2018), stating that the right to marry a person of one's choice is a part of Article 21.
  • Article 19: Protects freedoms like speech and assembly.
  • Article 25: Protects freedom of religion.
  • Article 29: Protects the interests of minorities.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
  1. The Parliament of India can place a particular law in the Ninth Schedule of the Constitution of India
  2. The validity of a law passed in the Ninth Schedule cannot be examined by any court and no judgment can be made on it
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Understanding the Ninth Schedule requires looking at both its text and judicial evolution:
  • Statement 1 is correct: Parliament has the power to place any law in the Ninth Schedule to shield it from judicial scrutiny.
  • Statement 2 is incorrect: In the I.R. Coelho v. State of Tamil Nadu (2007) case, the Supreme Court ruled that laws placed in the Ninth Schedule can be examined by the courts if they violate the Basic Structure of the Constitution or Fundamental Rights after April 24, 1973.

questions.question: Right to Privacy is protected as an intrinsic part of Right to Life and Personal Liberty. Which of the following in the Constitution of India correctly and appropriately imply the above statement?
questions.option_1: Article 14 and the provisions under the 42nd Amendment to the Constitution
questions.option_2: Article 17 and the Directive Principles of State Policy in Part IV
questions.option_3: Article 21 and freedom guaranteed in Part III
questions.option_4: Article 24 and provisions under the 44th Amendment to the Constitution
questions.solution:
The Right to Privacy is a multifaceted right primarily anchored in Article 21.
  • It is appropriately implied by Article 21 and the general freedoms guaranteed in Part III. The Puttaswamy judgement clarified that privacy is the bedrock of all liberties mentioned in Part III.
  • Other options link privacy to Article 14 (Equality), Article 17 (Untouchability), or Article 24 (Child Labour), which are not the primary constitutional sources for privacy rights.

questions.question: In the Federation established by The Government of India Act of 1935, residuary powers were given to the
questions.solution:
Under the Government of India Act, 1935, the distribution of powers was divided into Federal, Provincial, and Concurrent lists.
  • The Residuary Powers (subjects not mentioned in any list) were vested in the Governor-General of India.
  • In the current Constitution of India, residuary powers belong to the Union Parliament (Article 248).

questions.question:
Which of the following are regarded as the main features of the 'Rule of Law'?
  1. Limitation of powers
  2. Equality before law
  3. People's responsibility to the Government
  4. Liberty and civil rights
Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
questions.solution:
The 'Rule of Law', popularized by A.V. Dicey, has three main pillars:
  • Limitation of Powers: Government must act within the law (Constitutionalism).
  • Equality before Law: No one is above the law.
  • Liberty and Civil Rights: The law must protect individual rights.
  • Statement 3 is incorrect: Rule of Law emphasizes the Government's responsibility to the law and the people, not the 'People's responsibility to the Government' in a submissive sense.

questions.question: One of the implications of equality in society is the absence of
questions.solution:
In a social context, Equality primarily implies the absence of special privileges in favor of any individual or section of society.
  • If everyone is equal before the law, no one can claim special treatment based on birth, caste, or rank.
  • Restraints: Liberty is the absence of restraints; equality is about the status of individuals relative to each other.

questions.question: Which principle among the following was added to the Directive Principles of State Policy by the 42nd Amendment to the Constitution?
questions.option_1: Equal pay for equal work for both men and women
questions.option_2: Participation of workers in the management of industries
questions.option_3: Right to work, education, and public assistance
questions.option_4: Securing living wage and human conditions of work to workers
questions.solution:
The 42nd Amendment (1976) added several new Directive Principles:
  • Participation of workers in management: Added as Article 43A.
  • Other additions: Article 39A (Free legal aid), Article 39(f) (Healthy development of children), and Article 48A (Protection of environment).
  • Equal pay for equal work: This was already part of the original Article 39(d).

questions.question: Which one of the following statements is correct?
questions.option_1: Rights are claims of the State against the citizens
questions.option_2: Rights are privileges which are incorporated in the Constitution of a State
questions.option_3: Rights are claims of the citizens against the State
questions.option_4: Rights are privileges of a few citizens against the many
questions.solution:
In a democratic setup, Rights are best defined as claims of the citizens against the State.
  • They act as checks on the government and ensure that the state does not infringe upon the dignity and freedom of individuals.
  • Rights are not privileges (which are granted to a few) but are universal entitlements.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
  1. With reference to the Constitution of India, the Directive Principles of State Policy constitute limitations upon legislative function
  2. With reference to the Constitution of India, the Directive Principles of State Policy constitute limitations upon executive function
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Neither statement is correct.
  • Directive Principles (DPSP): These are affirmative instructions to the government to do something; they are not limitations.
  • Fundamental Rights: These act as limitations upon the legislative and executive functions of the State by telling them what not to do.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is/are true of the Fundamental Duties of an Indian citizen?
  1. A legislative process has been provided to enforce these duties
  2. They are correlative to legal duties
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.solution:
Neither statement is correct regarding the nature of Fundamental Duties (Part IV-A):
  • Statement 1: There is no direct legislative process to enforce them like a contract, though Parliament can pass separate laws (like the Wildlife Protection Act) to penalize specific actions.
  • Statement 2: They are non-justiciable. Unlike legal duties (which carry direct legal penalties for failure), Fundamental Duties are moral obligations and are not correlative to legal duties in a strict sense.

questions.question: Which one of the following objectives is not embodied in the Preamble to the Constitution of India?
questions.solution:
The Preamble mentions Liberty of Thought, Expression, Belief, Faith, and Worship.
  • Economic Liberty: This term is not mentioned in the Preamble. The Preamble mentions Economic Justice, but not 'liberty'.

questions.question: In the context of India, which one of the following is the correct relationship between Rights and Duties?
questions.option_2: Rights are personal and hence independent of society and Duties
questions.option_3: Rights, not Duties, are important for the advancement of the personality of the citizen
questions.option_4: Duties, not Rights, are important for the stability of the State
questions.solution:
In the Indian constitutional scheme, Rights and Duties are correlative.
  • This means they are two sides of the same coin; one person's right is another person's duty. The performance of duties by all is what creates the environment where rights can be enjoyed.

questions.question: The mind of the makers of the Constitution of India is reflected in which of the following?
questions.option_3: The Directive Principles of State Policy
questions.solution:
The Preamble serves as the 'key' to open the minds of the makers of the Constitution.
  • It contains the grand and noble vision of the Constituent Assembly and outlines the fundamental values and philosophy on which the Constitution is based.

questions.question: Right to vote and to be elected in India is a
questions.solution:
The Right to Vote in India is a Constitutional Right derived from Article 326.
  • While it is often discussed as a legal right (under the Representation of People Act), its root lies in the Constitution itself, which guarantees adult suffrage.

questions.question:
Which of the following are envisaged by the Right against Exploitation in the Constitution of India?
  1. Prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour
  2. Abolition of untouchability
  3. Protection of the interests of minorities
  4. Prohibition of employment of children in factories and mines
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.solution:
The Right against Exploitation consists of two specific Articles:
  • Article 23: Prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour (Statement 1).
  • Article 24: Prohibition of employment of children in factories/mines (Statement 4).
  • Untouchability: Covered under Right to Equality (Art 17).
  • Minority Interests: Covered under Cultural and Educational Rights (Art 29).

questions.question: The provisions in the Fifth Schedule and Sixth Schedule in the Constitution of India are made in order to:
questions.option_1: Protect the interests of Scheduled Tribes
questions.option_3: Determine the powers, authorities, responsibilities of Panchayats
questions.option_4: Protect the interests of all the border States
questions.solution:
The Fifth Schedule (Administration of Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes) and Sixth Schedule (Administration of Tribal Areas in Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, and Mizoram) are designed specifically to protect the interests of Scheduled Tribes.
  • They allow for a degree of autonomy and traditional self-governance to preserve tribal culture and prevent exploitation.

questions.question: Who/Which of the following is the Custodian of the Constitution of India?
questions.solution:
The Supreme Court of India is the Custodian and the final Interpreter of the Constitution.
  • It has the power of Judicial Review to strike down any law or executive action that violates the Constitution.
  • The President takes an oath to 'preserve, protect and defend' the Constitution, but the legal custody and interpretation lie with the Court.

questions.question: 'To uphold and protect the Sovereignty, Unity and Integrity of India' is a provision made in the
questions.solution:
The provision 'to uphold and protect the Sovereignty, Unity and Integrity of India' is explicitly listed as a Fundamental Duty under Article 51A(c).
  • While these terms appear in the Preamble, the specific phrase 'to uphold and protect' is the language used in the list of duties for citizens.

questions.question: The ideal of 'Welfare State' in the Indian Constitution is enshrined in its
questions.solution:
The ideal of a Welfare State is primarily enshrined in the Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP).
  • Articles 36 to 51 guide the State to ensure social and economic justice, which are the pillars of a welfare-oriented governance system.
